note:
对城市启动生态系统的影响在越来越多的企业家（许多人在城市中有很大面积的科技巨头工作）和投资者呼叫奥斯汀家园的营业型企业家（其中许多人）中有所引人注目。
 最近，Tesla计划在城市建立一个千兆和甲骨文的宣布，它在这里搬迁其总部已成为国家头条新闻。
  湾区比比皆是 - 从蓬勃发展和成熟的启动场景到过热和竞争的住房市场。
 虽然，风险投资家认为有几个关键的机会领域。在一点时，奥斯汀曾经是一个大型企业软件城市。虽然在这里软件仍然很大，但其他一些部门在包括CPG（消费品包装）和房地产科技等其他部门。
 以下是对城市中一些最高VC的调查及其关于奥斯汀生态系统的增长的预测。
   你在哪里看到奥斯汀的启动场景了五年来？这座城市多年来吸引了各种各样的人，包括最近的更大的技术和初创公司。它将如何加起来超过零件的总和？
 S3 Ventures的Eric工程师认为，到2030年，德克萨斯州的启动生态系统可能是该国的第二大第二大。 
“科技生态系统受益于良性周期。随着更有才华的人搬到德克萨斯州，更多的资本将遵循它们，然后将吸引更多人才，等等，“他说。
 16岁的Silverton Partners的摩根钞票人们表示，自从超过16年以来，Silverton的成立以来，该州最大的VC公司始终押注奥斯汀，更广泛地在德克萨斯州投注。
 “我们一直觉得如果奥斯汀赢得了，我们赢了，这就是我们如何运行我们的业务，”他说。 “但我们只是一个组织，现在我们加入了许多人在奥斯汀投注并希望他们成为使这个生态系统更好的东西......向前迈进，成功的关键组成部分是促进这一现有的社区合作哲学，这已经发挥了制作奥斯汀这样一个有吸引力的地方的一部分。奥斯汀是一个人们想要通过帮助他们的社区成功成功的地方，而且他们不愿意为后者牺牲前者。如果我们继续坚持那种核心哲学，我们就会成为一个惊人的未来。“
 首都工厂的约书亚·鲍尔认为，从现在起5年来，奥斯汀将从“升级”到“到达”，从一级城市的顶级到一个有竞争力的一级城市。他预测的两个大迹象，将是一个新的奥斯汀独角兽来自喜爱，Apptronik，迪斯科，鹰眼，Everlywell，图标和禅宗业务结合大系列的资金回合自从镇上只有一个大型VC公司叫做奥斯汀冒险的日子以来，城市尚未见过。
 非正统的企业创始逆机凯莉史密斯犹豫不决，以预测，并不难以努力，只是为了表征现场。
 “有这么多不同规模的公司做了这么多不同的东西，而且没有超越增长和创新的统一特征。它真的可以做任何方向，“他说。
 Liveoak Ventures的Srinivasa指出，德克萨斯州拥有巨大的财富500强公司，因此，具有能源，医疗保健，房地产，热情好客和零售等主要行业的深层域名。 
“这种域名知识当与大型技术和初创公司的汹涌技术人才交叉授粉已经具有繁殖效应，并进一步加速了创业活动，”他说。除了已经进行的大型技术人才和现在正在进行的现有启动生态系统的持续成熟，我们将继续在未来几年产生更大数量的令人难以置信的类别领导者。因此，我们觉得奥斯汀启动场景比以往任何时候都更快地增长。“
 下一个海岸企业的Floreani项目在五年内，奥斯汀将看到当前市场的成熟度的迹象，可能有一半的独角兽，终于公开了一半的初创公司。
 远程工作正在推动和拉动全球劳动力。这意味着一些办事处将从奥斯汀消失，即使有更多的公司进入，而且更多的当地人在其他地方远程工作。如何看到这些因素影响城市的技术演变？
 S3 Ventures的Eric工程师表示，才华横溢的人正在选择奥斯汀，因为它必须从生活方式的角度来看 - 不仅仅是这份工作。他认为，雷仪的工作可以缓解一些奥斯汀的日益增长的痛苦，因为工人每周少日勤。工程师说，这将使城市更多地开发额外的交通基础设施需要处理持续增长。
 Silverton Partners的旗帜思考遥远的工作改变了比赛领域一些，因为它允许工人在他们想要生活的地方更多选择，而他们必须生活在那里得到他们想要的工作。虽然它将给予人们更自由并使我们做得更高效的事情，但他认为大多数角色将需要一个混合解决方案，其中遥控工作是由人的互动补充。 “谢天谢地，奥斯汀都有两者 - 它是一个理想的生活地，它有一个迅速增长的科技界，”他说。 “这是我们看到此刻在这里移动的才能涌入的原因的一部分。”
 约书亚·贝尔认为，在任何地方都可以在任何地方工作，他们实际上不会到处工作。他认为将有大型赢家吸引了比其他地方更多的人才，而奥斯汀将成为未来5年的技术迁移中最大的赢家之一。 “更多公司将在这里移动他们的总部，更多的人会选择在这里工作，以便在这里为其他地方提供总部的办公室，”他预测。
 遥远的工作不适合非正统的企业的凯莉史密斯，他不相信它将为奥斯汀长期工作。当他在大流行的开始时从家里短暂工作时，他觉得与我工作的人相信 - 而且甚至是遥远的工作甚至忙碌，因为遥控器增加了“甚至是简单的任务甚至简单的任务”。 
“我真的茁壮成长，欣赏办公室的喧嚣。它让我们所有的动机都能让“史密斯说。 “简而言之，这是让我的轮子转向的润滑剂，我认为它需要在奥斯汀继续推动创新。”
 您在奥斯汀（及以后）内关注哪些行业部门？奥斯汀现在正在发生什么，你最兴奋的资金？
 在过去的15年中，S3企业主要投资B2B软件公司（其投资组合中的三分之二在这个空间中）。但它也在消费者数字经验和医疗保健中取得了成功的出口。
 “德克萨斯州经济令人难以置信的经济，因此技术创新也是广泛的，”工程师表示。 “我们正在看到巨大的经济部门（金融服务，房地产，医疗，教育，熟练的交易，热情好客，能源，制造业），即数字化，现在开始在整个组织中接受基于云的解决方案。“
 Silverton Partners一直是一般基金。也就是说，根据旗子，在坚硬的那一刻，存在几个脱位发生了几个脱位。具体而言，他说，数字健康，金融气/芦荟和教育的迅速干扰。 “这些大规模产业中的剧本正在被重写，我们肯定正在寻找具有相对于未来看起来的强烈观点的企业家，”彩信补充道。
 资本厂的约书亚·贝尔认为，这座城市的创业公司的各种场景是使其如此充满活力的一部分。 “如果它在技术中很热，那就发生在奥斯汀，”他说。最近的一些地区包括人工智能和机器学习，消费者包装的商品，无人机，电子商务，医疗保健，市场，机器人和萨斯。
 非正统企业的Carey Smith表示，他的公司专注于投资公司制作解决实际问题的有形产品的公司。与城市中的许多其他VC不同，虽然这是奥斯汀文化的重要组成部分，但他不是软件的粉丝。 “我更喜欢制造消费品的挑战，”他说。 “除此之外，软件公司缺乏多样性，只有在他们创造的工作中有利于社会的小块。另一方面，制造，帮助每个人从GED到PHDS。“ 
Liveoak Venture Partners在几乎所有行业投资。在过去的一年中，它已经支持了Proptech，Fintech，零售，供应链，业务合规，网络安全和Edtech的公司。
 特别是Provtech，令人兴奋的生活，而且这座城市已经取得了成功，如OPCity和OJO实验室。另一个具有巨大潜力的领域是法律/遵守的初创公司。迪斯科和米特拉特科是总部位于奥斯汀的大公司，令人兴奋的奥斯塔诺，entenus和Litlingo等快速发展的新人。
 对于下海岸的Floreani，奥斯汀击中规模的成功B2C初创公司的成功数量增加是有希望的，“由于它将我们的人才池和秘密的秘密酱在奥斯汀的早期被称为半导体和SaaS城镇。”
 “我们仍然有很棒的软件企业家，但如果您认为Everlywell，文人，Flosports，氛围，氛围，我们也有DNA进行消费者和媒体。
 您遇到或看到的创始人斗争是什么？更一般地说，人们应该如何雇用，投资或搬迁到奥斯汀考虑在城市做生意？
 当地投资者之间的一般共识是，奥斯汀的科技界是欢迎和协作，他们都以任何想到搬到城市的人的资源作为资源。 S3 Ventures的工程师表示，他的公司已经有几个来自两个海岸的移植分享如何对这种文化感到惊喜 - 特别是与他们习惯的东西相比。
 Silverton的纸卷承认，移动始终是一个具有挑战性的事件，但在Covid期间移动肯定是自己的一类。 
“我已经看到一些创始人在搬到大流行中搬到奥斯汀后找到强大的社区关系，”他说。 “几乎所有的一切都是虚拟的，与新的生态系统更加困难，这使得整合。我作为奥斯汀生态系统的投资者和管家的一部分是促进创始人社区内的更多连接。随着疫苗卷展栏继续，人们的联系人变得更加安全，Silverton计划举办一系列社区建筑活动，以更好地整合创始人在这里移动。“
 国会大厦工厂的轰烧认为德克萨斯州投资者比硅谷的同龄人更多地考虑预防。他对公司的建议？发展两种不同的音高，知道你是谁。 “德克萨斯州的音乐谈论你如何破坏业务和硅谷音调谈论，如果它有效，这可能是多大的，”他说。
 对于非正统的企业，史密斯建议创始人在奥斯汀或其他地方小心组织，这些组织在“持续”时，希望帮助企业家，而“持续到以回报为合理地证明”。“
 “创始人需要确保他们对投资者和顾问有选择，他们选择的投资者和顾问以及他们选择不仅仅是首都的人，”他说。
 Liveoak Ventures的Srinivasa警告奥斯汀的人并不是交易，如果你遇到交易，并且严格与某人以交易方式和关系方式互动，那么它就错误的方式摩擦了人们。“
 “当他们来到这里时，局外人需要意识到这一点，”他说。 “在招聘决定时，选择一个良好的文化契合的人是很重要的，而不是完全基于恢复雇用。从投资者的角度来看，如果您想在这里做生意，除了提供有吸引力的交易之外，重点建立强大的关系很重要。“
 下一个海岸企业的Floreani指出，招聘增长领导者在她公司的投资组合中一直是一个一致的挑战。 “如果奥斯汀有一个更深的营销和销售领导者，那就太好了，”她说。 
谁是关键的初创人，您在本地创建成功，是否投资者，创始人甚至其他类型的创业生态系统，如律师，设计师，增长专家等我们正试图突出推动者和散步者可能不知道的搬运工。
 S3：创始人/首席执行官 -  Leo Resig（大气），Jag Bath（恩惠），Kim Rodriguez（Acessa），Doug Donovan（Interward Learning），Richard Lebovitz（Leandna），Adelle Archer（Eterneva），Andy Ambrose（Liveoak Technology）， Adam Berman（TVA Medical），Dion Cornett（Liquibase）
 投资者 -  Rosa McCormick（野生盆地），Oksana Malysheva（Sputnik），Adam Lipman（Edliptic Capital），以及Josh Baer / Bryan Chambers（Capital Factory）
 服务提供商 -  Marc Nathan（E / N），John Gump（CBRE），Lathrop Smith（MLR），奥斯汀威利斯（SVB），Paul O'Brien（Mediatech）
 Silverton：Heather Brunner和Jason Cohen @ WP发动机，查克戈登和马里奥·菲戈·@ Sceparefoot，Jess Ewing @ Lentrati，Blake Garret @acable，James Garvey @ Self Financial，Brian Cruver @ Alert Media，John Banczak和T.J. Clark @ Transkey，Michelle Davey @ Wheel，Rob Taylor @ Catch和其他几个。
  非正统的企业：Christa Freeland建于ATX套件，这是一个支持超过40名当地食品企业家的创业公司，其奥斯汀中心的小吃盒。
 鲍勃桥和西南天使网络（SWAN）支持旨在解决严重的社会挑战，包括环境，健康和社会司法问题。 
Liveoak：我们很幸运能在我们的网络中有许多搬运工和摇摇晃晃。我们不希望以牺牲另一个代价包含一个;据说，我们要认识到丹格雷厄姆，通过光束天使网络推出了投资基金的恩克利风险资助的社会影响初创公司，并在CPG的出现中发挥了积极作用。 Jim Breyer只有一年，在这一时期，在奥斯汀，当地初创公司和这个城镇的能量方面是一个令人难以置信的倡导者。这是一个很大的积极添加。
 NextCoast Ventures：这些初创公司的创始人对Austin具有令人难以置信的增长计划：Everlywell，Upequity，Serteade，Eterneva，Lentrati，Flosports和Enboarder。请注意，我可能有点偏见，因为我们已经支持的大多数公司。
 您如何看待硅谷的比较？您认为（大＆amp;小）科技公司的涌入是什么影响？
 S3的工程师相信只有一个硅谷，并且在西方世界上有任何可比性。
 但他认为，今天今天越来越小，如果趋势在未来十年中持续下来，德克萨斯州生态系统在生长的情况下具有比纽约和波士顿更大的大。
 Silverton的纸卷在硅谷出生并养成。他去那里上学，也在那里工作。结果，他不是比较的忠实粉丝。
 “每个地方都与不同的利弊截然不同，”他说。 “奥斯汀将在大小和活动方面超越硅谷吗？我真的不知道 - 我们肯定有很长的路要走。在某种程度上，我不确定很重要。“ 
他所关心的是奥斯汀保留了它的充满活力的性质，其目前的增长丰富了城市，而不是稀释其能量。 “重要的是要认识到奥斯汀的文化尚未以科技为中心，”格兰特说。 “奥斯汀是一家独特的现场音乐，美食，艺术，户外生活和”保持奇怪“的巨大动力。
 他补充说：“正如我思考我对硅谷的钦佩和奥斯汀的所有东西都需要做到这一点，我倾向于花费尽可能多的时间考虑我们可以不同的事情。”
 非正统的企业“史密斯认为，”每个人都想离开硅谷“并不奇怪。
 “他们终于想到了这是一场灾难，”他说。 “作为更聪明，勤奋和好奇的人聚集在这里，这对奥斯汀和更广泛的VC来说是一件好事。硅谷的问题之一是，这种强大的VC金钱百分比留在那里或纽约或波士顿。我们在奥斯汀和整个中美洲的其他创新城市中需要更多的资金，我们正在为日常美国人解决真正的问题。由于奥斯汀感觉越来越多地从硅谷影响了硅谷，这对于基于技术为导向的项目来说非常重要。风险资本家也需要专注于基本的人类需求。“
 Liveoak的Srinivasa知道COMPARISONS对硅谷是不可避免的，并且在大多数情况下，当这些比较为奥斯汀带来更广泛的国家和国际重视并加强了一个拥有蓬勃发展的初创公司的城市的声誉。
 “在这里有很多活力和势头到了硅谷的早期希望 - 越来越多的业务，正在推出的越来越多的企业，资助的公司数量，这里流动的人才渴望雕刻自己科技界，“他说。 “然而我们知道奥斯汀有一些独一无二的东西，使它与任何其他技术中心 - 这里的人民的协同精神相结合。这个社区也有一个可触及的能量和兴奋。奥斯汀对硅谷提出的成功致敬，我们将融入他们的积极方面，同时锻造自己的道路。“
 下海岸的Floreani认为，比较已经过度使用了十年。 “我们不是硅谷，永远不会是它，”她说。 “我不是说在负面意义上。我在海湾地区长大。我只是相信我们在这里以不同的方式种植公司，尽管更多的SV人才和投资者来到城镇，但我认为我们不会突然转向更多的山谷，就像闪电类这样的成长公司一样。相反，我认为我们会发现一个融合快速增长和可持续性的快乐媒介。“
note_en:
The impact on the city’s startup ecosystem is noticeable in the growing number of entrepreneurs (many of whom worked at some of the tech giants who have a big presence in the city) and investors calling Austin home.
 Most recently, Tesla’s plans to build a gigafactory in the city and Oracle’s announcement that it was relocating its headquarters here have made national headlines.
  The comparisons to the Bay Area abound — from the burgeoning and maturing startup scene to an overheated and competitive housing market.
 All the while, venture capitalists see several key areas of opportunities. At one point, Austin was once a big enterprise software city. While software is still big here, a number of other sectors are growing including CPG (consumer packaged goods) and real estate tech, among others.
 What follows is a survey of some of the top VCs in the city and their projections about the growth of Austin’s ecosystem.
   Where do you see Austin’s startup scene five years from now? The city has attracted a wide range of people over the years, including more big tech and startups very recently. How will it add up to something more than the sum of the parts?
 Eric Engineer of S3 Ventures believes that by 2030, the Texas startup ecosystem could be the country’s second largest.
 “Tech ecosystems benefit from virtuous cycles. As more talented people move to Texas, more capital will follow them, which will then attract more talent, and so on,” he said.
 Morgan Flager of 16-year-old Silverton Partners says since Silverton’s inception more than 16 years ago, the state’s largest VC firm has always bet on Austin and, more broadly, on Texas.
 “We’ve always felt that if Austin wins, we win, and that’s how we’ve run our business,” he says. “But we are just one organization, and now we are joined by many others who are also betting on Austin and want to do their part to make this ecosystem better…Moving forward, a critical component of success is fostering this existing philosophy of community collaboration, which has already played a part in making Austin such an attractive place to be. Austin is a place where people want to succeed by helping their community succeed, and they aren’t willing to sacrifice the former for the latter. If we continue to hold on to that core philosophy as we grow, we’re in for an amazing future.”
 Joshua Baer of Capital Factory believes that in 5 years from now, Austin will have gone from “up and coming” to “arrived” and from the top of the Tier 2 cities to a competitive Tier 1 city. Two of the big signs of this, he predicted, will be a new cohort of Austin unicorns from the likes of Aceable, Apptronik, Disco, Eagle Eye, Everlywell, ICON, and Zen Business combined with big Series A funding rounds of the likes the city has not seen since the days when there was only one big VC firm in town called Austin Ventures.
 Unorthodox Ventures Founding Contrarian Carey Smith hesitates to predict, noting that it’s hard enough just to characterize the scene as it is right now.
 “There are so many companies of so many different sizes doing so many different things, and there isn’t really a unifying characteristic beyond growth and innovation. It really could go any direction,” he says.
 LiveOak Ventures’ Srinivasa points out that Texas has tremendous number of Fortune 500 companies and, as a result, folks with deep domain in major industries such as energy, healthcare, real estate, hospitality and retail.
 “This domain knowledge when cross-pollinated with the surging tech talent from big tech and startups has already had a multiplicative effect and is further accelerating entrepreneurial activity,” he said. Besides big tech talent and the continued maturation of the current startup ecosystem that is already underway, we will all continue to spawn an even greater number of incredible category leaders in the years to come. As such, we feel the Austin startup scene is poised to grow faster than ever before.”
 Floreani of Next Coast Ventures projects that in  five years, Austin will see signs of a maturity of the current market with potentially half a dozen unicorns and half a dozen startups finally going public.
 Remote work is pushing and pulling the global workforce. This means that some offices will disappear from Austin, even with more companies moving in, but also more locals who work remotely for companies elsewhere. How do you see these factors impacting the city’s tech evolution?
 Eric Engineer of S3 Ventures says that talented people are choosing Austin because of all it has to offer from a lifestyle perspective – not just the job. He believes r emote work could ease some of Austin’s growing pains as workers will commute fewer days per week. That will give the city more time to develop the additional transportation infrastructure it needs to handle the continued growth, Engineer said.
 Silverton Partners’ Flager thinks remote work shifts the playing field some because it allows workers more choice with respect to where they want to live versus where they have to live to get the job they want.  And while it will give people more freedom and make certain things we do more efficient, he thinks most roles will require a hybrid solution where remote work is complemented by in-person interactions. “Thankfully, Austin has both — it is a desirable place to live and it has a rapidly growing tech community,” he said. “This is part of the reason we’re seeing such an influx of talent moving here at the moment.”
 Joshua Baer believes that wh en people can work anywhere, they won’t actually work everywhere. He thinks there will be big winners that attract much more talent than other places, and that Austin will be one of the biggest winners in the tech migration of the next 5 years. “More companies will move their headquarters here, and more people will choose to work here for offices that have headquarters elsewhere,” he predicts.
 Remote work doesn’t work for Unorthodox Ventures’ Carey Smith and he doesn’t believe it will work for Austin long-term. When he briefly worked from home at the start of the pandemic, he felt far less connected to the people I work with — and yet was even busier as remote work added “so much difficulty to even simple tasks.”
 “I really thrive with face-to-face meetings and appreciate the hustle and bustle of the office. It keeps all of us motivated,” Smith said. “In short, it’s the lubricant that keeps my wheels turning, and I think it’s required to keep driving innovation in Austin.”
 What industry sectors do you focus on within Austin (and beyond)? What is happening in Austin now that you’re most excited to fund?
 Over the past 15 years, S3 Ventures has primarily investing in B2B software companies (two-thirds of its portfolio are in this space). But it has also had successful exits in consumer digital experiences and healthcare.
 “Texas has an incredibly diverse economy, and so the technology innovation is also broad-based,” Engineer said. “We are seeing huge sectors of the economy (financial services, real estate, health care, education, skilled trades, hospitality, industrial, energy, manufacturing) that were slower to digitize, now start to embrace cloud-based solutions across their entire organizations.”
 Silverton Partners has always been a generalist fund. That said, there are several dislocations occurring at the moment that are hard for the firm to ignore, according to Flager. Specifically, he said, there’s rapid disruption in digital health, fintech /insurtech, and education. “The playbooks in these massive industries are being rewritten and we are certainly looking for entrepreneurs who have a strong point of view relative to what the future will look like,” Flager added.
 Capital Factory’s Joshua Baer believes the variety of the city’s startup scene is part of what makes it so vibrant. “ If it’s hot in tech, it’s happening in Austin,” he said. Some of the areas that have been big recently include artificial intelligence and machine learning, consumer packaged goods, drones, e-commerce, healthcare, marketplaces, robotics and SaaS.
 Unorthodox Ventures’ Carey Smith said his firm is focused on investing in companies making tangible products that solve real problems. And unlike many other VCs in the city, and even though it’s a big part of the culture in Austin, he’s not a fan of software. “I prefer the challenge of manufacturing consumer goods,” he said. “Beyond that, software companies lack diversity and only benefit a small sliver of society with the jobs they create. Manufacturing, on the other hand, helps everyone from GEDs to PhDs.”
 LiveOak Venture Partners  invests across pretty much all industries. In the past year, it has backed companies in proptech, fintech, retail, supply chain, business compliance, cyber security and edtech.
 Proptech, in particular, is exciting to LiveOak, and the city has seen success in companies such as Opcity and OJO Labs. Another area with big potential are startups working in legal/compliance. Disco and Mitratech are large companies headquartered in Austin and there are exciting fast growing newcomers like Osano, Eventus and Litlingo.
 For Next Coast’s Floreani, the increased number of successful B2C startups in Austin hitting scale is promising “as it cements our talent pool and secret sauce beyond Austin’s early days of being know as a semiconductor and SaaS town.”
 “We still have great software entrepreneurs but we also have the DNA to do consumer and media if you consider EverlyWell, Literati, FloSports, Atmosphere, to name a few,” she said.
 What are some of the local challenges you’ve encountered or seen founders struggle with? More generally, how should people looking to hire in, invest in, or relocate to Austin think about doing business in the city?
 The general consensus among local investors is that Austin’s tech community is welcoming and collaborative and that they all went to serve as a resource to anyone thinking of moving to the city. S3 Ventures’ Engineer said his firm has had several recent transplants from both coasts share how they were pleasantly surprised by this culture – especially compared to what they were used to.
 Silverton’s Flager acknowledges that moving is always a challenging event, but moving during COVID is certainly a class of its own.
 “I’ve seen some founders struggle with finding strong community ties after relocating to Austin in the midst of a pandemic,” he said. “Almost everything is virtual and that makes integration with a new ecosystem more difficult. Part of my role as an investor and steward of the Austin ecosystem is to facilitate more connectivity within the founder community. As the vaccine rollouts continue and in-person contact becomes safer, Silverton is planning to host a series of community building events to better integrate founders moving here.”
 Capitol Factory’s Baer believes  Texas investors think about loss prevention more than their peers in Silicon Valley. His advice to companies? Develop two different pitches and know who you are pitching to. “The Texas pitch talks about how you have de-risked the business and the Silicon Valley pitch talks about how big this could be if it works,” he said.
 For Unorthodox Ventures’  Smith advises founders to be careful of organizations in Austin or elsewhere  that look to help entrepreneurs while “taking equity and not giving enough in return to justify it.”
 “Founders need to make sure they are selective about the investors and advisers they take on and that they choose people who offer more than just capital,” he said.
 LiveOak Ventures’ Srinivasa warns that people in Austin  are not transactional and that if you come across as being transactional and strictly interact with somebody in a transactional way and not a relational way, “it rubs people the wrong way.”
 “Outsiders need to be aware of this when they come here,” he said. “When making a hiring decision, it’s important to choose someone who is a good cultural fit, and not hire based solely on resume. From an investor perspective, if you want to do business here, it’s important to focus building strong relationships, besides offering attractive deal terms.”
 Next Coast Ventures’ Floreani notes that hiring growth leaders has been a consistent challenge across her firm’s portfolio. “It would be great if Austin had a deeper bench of talented marketing and sales leaders,” she said.
 Who are key startup people you see creating success locally, whether investors, founders or even other types of startup ecosystems roles like lawyers, designers, growth experts, etc. We’re trying to highlight the movers and shakers who outsiders might not know.
 S3: Founders/CEOs – Leo Resig (Atmosphere), Jag Bath (Favor), Kim Rodriguez (Acessa), Doug Donovan (Interplay Learning), Richard Lebovitz (LeanDNA), Adelle Archer (Eterneva), Andy Ambrose (LiveOak Technology), Adam Berman (TVA Medical), Dion Cornett (Liquibase)
 Investors – Rosa McCormick (Wild Basin), Oksana Malysheva (Sputnik), Adam Lipman (Ecliptic Capital), and Josh Baer / Bryan Chambers (Capital Factory)
 Service Providers – Marc Nathan (E/N), John Gump (CBRE), Lathrop Smith (MLR), Austin Willis (SVB), Paul O’Brien (Mediatech)
 Silverton: Heather Brunner and Jason Cohen @ WP Engine, Chuck Gordon and Mario Feghali @ Sparefoot, Jess Ewing @ Literati, Blake Garrett @Aceable, James Garvey @ Self Financial, Brian Cruver @ Alert Media, John Banczak and T.J. Clark @ TurnKey, Michelle Davey @ Wheel, Rob Taylor @ Convey and several others.
  Unorthodox Ventures: Christa Freeland built  ATX Kit, a startup that supports more than 40 local food entrepreneurs with her Austin-centric snack boxes.
 Bob Bridge and the Southwest Angel Network (SWAN) support startups aiming to solve serious societal challenges, including environmental, health and social justice issues.
 LiveOak:   We are fortunate to have many movers and shakers here in our network. We do not wish to include one at the expense of another; that being said, we do want to recognize Dan Graham, who funds social impact startups through Notley Ventures, who has launched a fund to invest in women entrepreneurs, via the BEAM Angel Network and has played an active role in the emergence of CPG. Jim Breyer has been here for only a year and in that period has been an incredible advocate in op-eds, blog posts and interviews for Austin, the local startups and the energy of this town. That has been a big positive addition.
 NextCoast Ventures:  The founders of these startups have incredible growth plans for Austin: EverlyWell, Upequity, Steadily, Eterneva, Literati, FloSports, and Enboarder. Please note I may be a bit biased here as most of these are companies we have backed.
 What do you think of comparisons to Silicon Valley? What impact do you think the influx of (big & small) tech companies is having on the city’s startup scene?
 S3’s Engineer believes there is only one Silicon Valley, and that it is highly unlikely there will be anything comparable in the Western world.
 But he thinks that while much smaller today, if the trends continue over the next ten years, the Texas ecosystem has a real shot at growing as large, if not larger, than New York and Boston.
 Silverton’s Flager was born and raised in Silicon Valley. He went to school there and worked there as well. As a result, he’s not a big fan of the comparison.
 “Each place is distinct with different pros and cons,” he said. “Will Austin ever overtake Silicon Valley in terms of size and activity? I really don’t know — we certainly have a long way to go. To some extent, I’m not sure that matters.”
 What he does care about is that Austin retains its vibrant character and that its current growth enriches the city, rather than dilutes its energy. “It’s important to recognize that Austin’s culture has not been tech-centric,” Flager said. “Austin is a unique powerhouse of live music, great food, arts, outdoor living and ‘keeping it weird.’ ”
 He adds: “As I contemplate what I admire about Silicon Valley and all the things Austin needs to do to be at that level, I tend to spend as much time thinking about what we could do differently.”
 Unorthodox Ventures’ Smith believes it’s no surprise that “ everyone wants to leave Silicon Valley.
 “They finally figured out it’s a disaster,” he said. “As more intelligent, hardworking and curious people gather here, it’s a good thing for Austin and for VCs more broadly. One of the problems with Silicon Valley is that such a strong percentage of VC money stays there or in New York or Boston. We need more capital in Austin and other innovative cities throughout Middle America where we’re solving real problems for the everyday American. As Austin feels more and more influence from Silicon Valley, it’s so important to fund more than tech-oriented projects. Venture capitalists need to focus on basic human needs, too.”
 LiveOak’s Srinivasa knows that c omparisons to Silicon Valley are inevitable, and for the most part welcome when those comparisons bring broader national and international attention to Austin and reinforces its reputation as a city with a booming startup scene.
 “There’s a lot of vibrancy and momentum to the startup scene here that is reminiscent of the early days of Silicon Valley — the growing number businesses being launched, the number of companies being funded, the amount of talent flowing here eager to engrain themselves in the tech community,” he said. “Yet we know there is something unique to Austin that sets it apart from any other tech hub—the collaborative spirit of the people here. There’s also an energy and excitement in this community that’s palpable. Austin has great respect for the successes Silicon Valley has brought forth, and we will incorporate their positive aspects, while forging our own path.”
 Next Coast’s Floreani believes that comparison has been overused for a decade. “We are not Silicon Valley and will never be it,” she says. “I am not saying that in a negative sense. I grew up in the Bay Area. I simply believe we grow companies differently here and even though more SV talent and investors are coming to town I don’t think we will suddenly shift to a more Valley like approach to growing companies like blitz-scaling. Rather I think we will find a happy medium that incorporates fast growth and sustainability.”

note_en:
Hello and welcome back to  Equity, TechCrunch’s venture capital-focused podcast, where we unpack the numbers behind the headlines.
 Natasha and  Danny and  and  Grace were all here to chat through the week’s rigamarole of news.  Alex took some well-deserved time off, but that meant we got to poke a little fun at him and create a Special Edition segment to start off the show.
  Jokes aside, this week was yet another spree of creator economy, edtech, and new fund announcements, with fresh and unexpected news hailing from Natasha’s home state, New Jersey.
  Box got a lifeline in the form of a  $500 million check from KKR, and as Danny mentions, Box CEO Aaron Levie shifts his role a bit, too.
 Patreon, an early startup in the creator economy space,  has tripled its valuation to $4 billion. Another, not-so-old startup with the same reported valuation? Clubhouse. We threaded the line between the two, and gave some color on  a new monetization feature rolled out.
 As an aside, looks like Clubhouse is  everyone’s favorite app to tweet about so much so that even Twitter is reportedly considering an acquisition.
 MasterClass raising new funding at $2.5 billion valuation. That is up from a $800 million valuation just last year, and shows that aspirational teaching packaged as celebrity-taught Youtube-like videos with Netflix-like quality is enough to be considered edtech.
 Speaking of edtech, a series of exits have caught our attention.  The consolidation has begun!
 We spoke about two new funds. First,  New Jersey is proposing a $10 million fund that would go to Black and LatinX founders. Second,  Index raised nine-figures for seed startups, beating Sequoia’s seed fund by a measly $5 million.
 The show closed with two early-stage funding rounds you don’t want to miss –  Walnut and  Real – as well as a conversation on the future of telehealth.
 What a show! We’ll be back with the full trio next week, and until then, stay safe and thank you for listening.
 Equity drops every Monday at 7:00 a.m. PST, Wednesday, and Friday at 6:00 AM PST, so subscribe to us on   Apple Podcasts ,   Overcast ,   Spotify  and all the casts!

note_en:
Apple knows that iMessage’s blue bubbles are a big barrier to people switching to Android, which is why the service has never appeared on Google’s mobile operating system. That’s according to depositions and emails from Apple employees, including some high-ranking executives, revealed in  a court filing from Epic Games  as part of its legal dispute with the iPhone manufacturer.
 Epic argues that Apple consciously tries to lock customers into its ecosystem of devices, and that iMessage is one of the key services helping it to do so. It cites comments made by Apple’s senior vice president of Internet Software and Services Eddie Cue, senior vice president of software engineering Craig Federighi, and Apple Fellow Phil Schiller to support its argument.
  “The #1 most difficult [reason] to leave the Apple universe app is iMessage ... iMessage amounts to serious lock-in,” was how one unnamed former Apple employee put it in an email in 2016, prompting Schiller to respond that, “moving iMessage to Android will hurt us more than help us, this email illustrates why.”
 “iMessage on Android would simply serve to remove [an] obstacle to iPhone families giving their kids Android phones”
 “iMessage on Android would simply serve to remove [an] obstacle to iPhone families giving their kids Android phones,” was Federighi’s concern according to the Epic filing. Although workarounds to using iMessage on Android have emerged over the years, none have been particularly convenient or reliable.
 According to Epic’s filing, citing Eddie Cue, Apple decided not to develop iMessage for Android as early as 2013, following the launch of the messaging service with iOS 5 in 2011. Cue admits that Apple “could have made a version on Android that worked with iOS” so that “users of both platforms would have been able to exchange messages with one another seamlessly.” Evidently, such a version was never developed.
 Along with iMessage, Epic cites a series of other Apple services that it argues contribute to lock-in. Notably, these include its video chat service FaceTime, which Steve Jobs announced  would be an open industry standard back at WWDC 2010. FaceTime subsequently released across iPhones, iPads, and Macs, but it’s not officially available for any non-Apple devices.

今天，您可以在Netflix上装入一部电影，然后在一分钟内捕捉优步，并在一小时内送杂货。你认为金钱会尽快移动 - 它的一些情况。但大多数情况下，或者在美国，每年约62万亿美元，比汽车慢得多，需要5天才能安全到达。这就是为什么消费者通常需要等待几天，因为在注册抢劫队或数字银行时可以在抢劫之后才能在罗马时代举办第一步。
   奥卢姆，一个大约40名员工的纽约初创公司，旨在将货币缩小到24小时。它筹集了2100万美元的贝恩首都领导的“系列”资金。风险公司启发了资本，自制，ACREW，小学，钟表，箱子集团，AMEX企业和SVB资本也投资。
 Stephany Kirkpatrick于2019年创办Oreum，在学习工作七年后，金融规划Fintech公司由Alexa Von Tobel开始，由2015年以3.75亿美元的3.75亿美元获得西北互联网。经过认证的金融计划者Kirkpatrick，从亚利桑那州开业的首次呼叫中心进行了帮助，以帮助设计其财务规划软件 - 她的名字出现在其专利上。
 “在学习中，我看到六到七百万人获得财务计划，也许10％执行它。这是为什么？”这位39岁的奥卢姆首席执行官说。 “它＆＃39;我们的金融系统中有如此多的摩擦。人们不想把钱放在高产储蓄中，即使它＆＃39;更好的速度，因为他们＆＃39;如果他们有紧急情况，他们就可以快速解决它足够的。我迷恋那个问题。“ Von Tobel是Kirkpatrick的第一个支持者，通过她的VC公司启发资本投资。 “这是一种巨大的，复杂，肉质的问题，困扰了数千个机构，”弗别特说。
 Kirkpatrick开始orum，因为这么多的钱慢慢移动，但有些交易很快就会解决。在线打印在网上或在商店滑动它，零售商几乎立即知道是否经历过。卡网络速度快，而且昂贵的零售商也在接受卡时咳嗽2％的销售额。相比之下，通过自动化结算房屋（ACH）网络转移的任何东西，例如您的薪水，自动账单付款和银行转移，通常只需0.30美元，但最多需要五天。 ACH于1974年推出，由金融机构资助的非营利组织纳哈开展。
 除了缓慢，ACH还具有很少的数据。当有人签署Chime和请求从她的Wells Fargo账户转移2,000美元时，最初没有信息判断该人是否实际上在她的Wells Fargo账户中有2,000美元。因此，它等待交易来满足于为新帐户提供资金。
 为了通过金融机构的伙伴关系弥合这一信息差距，ORUM的“远见”产品通过伙伴关系提取额外数据，并使用机器学习来评估交易的风险。例如，ORUM可以告诉客户给定的银行转账是99％可能是安全的。收到此信息很快让您的收到银行向消费者提供更快的日常资金 - 通常是同一天 - 并提供更好的用户体验。 “过去五年的机器学习的出现使得这项[快速风险评估]可能，”Kirkpatrick说。 
Oreum的15个客户包括数字银行Sofi和基于孟菲斯的157岁的银行第一地平线，已经加快了85％的存款转移，因此它们在一天中完成，而通常它通常在三到四天内完成需要。他们还观察到“NSF”或基金不足的情况下减少了40％。 NSF是客户试图搬运金钱的时候，但随着交易即将完成的时间，在账户中没有足够的款项留下了反弹检查。 NSFS对每个人都不糟糕，通常为消费者触发35美元的费用，金融机构的5美元费用，如果它们经常发生，导致NACHA削减银行或Fintech对ACH网络的访问。
 ORUM的第二个产品，“动量”就像一个控制中心，指导经销轨道交易应该在鉴于金融机构的速度和成本的偏好方面旅行。当消费者启动转移时，势头智能地路由，无论是在ACP，信用卡网络，银行的新实时支付网络甚至最终，最终都是基于区块链的结算系统。
 ORUM于2020年9月与其第一个客户一起生活，并在一个月内加工数千万美元的交易。其客户为服务每月支付5,000美元至25,000美元的价格。
 纽约公司仍处于早期阶段，面临着许多挑战。 Kirkpatrick说，与金融机构的技术融合可能是复杂的，并且必须与Oreum的技术团队“超过一系列Sprints”。 orum有50个客户的候补名单。
 如果美国政府推出了央行数字货币或“数字美元”，可能会出现另一个挑战，或者“数字美元”在美国大大加快了美国的付款，减少了对羊眼的需求。
 Matt Harris是博亚投资者和Bain Capital的合作伙伴，表示数字美元的“最具侵略性”实施将是美国所有美国人在美联储的银行账户，并且可以立即搬家。哈里斯说，成千上万的金融服务公司将面临风险。我们不再需要银行进行现金管理，签证或万事达卡付款，venmo进行金钱转移。 “我认为这是＆＃39;＆＃39;艾滋病的想法，”哈里斯说。 “他们说他们不在这里摧毁银行业。”他认为数字元账户将限制为几个目的，如政府资金支付刺激支付。

note_en:
Today you can load a movie on Netflix in a second, catch an Uber in a minute and get groceries delivered in an hour. You’d think money would move just as quickly—and some of it does. But most of it, or about $62 trillion a year in the U.S., travels more slowly than a car, taking up to five days to arrive safely. That’s why consumers usually need to wait several days before they can make their first transaction after signing up for Robinhood or a digital bank like Chime.
   Orum, a New York startup with about 40 employees, aims to shrink money movement times down to 24 hours. It has raised $21 million in “Series A” funding led by Bain Capital. Venture firms Inspired Capital, Homebrew, Acrew, Primary, Clocktower, Box Group, Amex Ventures and SVB Capital also invested.
 Stephany Kirkpatrick founded Orum in 2019 after working for seven years at LearnVest, the financial planning fintech company  started by Alexa von Tobel and acquired by Northwestern Mutual in 2015 for $375 million. Kirkpatrick, a certified financial planner, did everything from opening LearnVest’s first call center in Arizona to helping design its financial planning software—her name appears on its patents.
 “At LearnVest I saw six to seven million people get a financial plan and maybe 10% execute it. Why is that?” the 39-year-old Orum CEO says. “It's because there’s so much friction in our financial system. People don't want to put money in high-yield savings, even though it's a better rate, because they're afraid that if they have an emergency, they can't get it out fast enough. I became obsessed with that problem.” Von Tobel was Kirkpatrick’s first backer, investing through her VC firm Inspired Capital. “This is a massive, complicated, meaty problem that plagues thousands of institutions,” von Tobel says.
 Kirkpatrick started Orum because so much money moves slowly, but some transactions settle quickly. Punch in your credit card online or swipe it at a store, and a retailer knows almost instantly whether it went through. Card networks are fast, but also expensive—retailers cough up 2% of their sales when they accept cards. By contrast, anything transferred over the Automated Clearing House (ACH) network, such as your paycheck, automated bill payments and bank transfers, often costs just $0.30 but takes up to five days. ACH launched in 1974 and is run by Nacha, a nonprofit association funded by financial institutions.
 In addition to being slow, ACH carries very little data. When someone signs up for Chime and requests to transfer $2,000 from her Wells Fargo account, Chime initially has little information to judge whether this person actually has $2,000 in her Wells Fargo account. So it waits for the transaction to settle to fund the new account.
 To bridge this information gap, Orum’s “Foresight” product pulls in extra data through partnerships with financial institutions and uses machine learning to assess the risk of a transaction. For example, Orum can tell its customers that a given bank transfer is 99% likely to be safe. Receiving this information quickly lets the receiving bank advance funds to a consumer much faster—often the same day—and deliver a better user experience. “The advent of machine learning over the past five years made this [fast risk assessment] possible,” Kirkpatrick says.
  Orum’s 15 clients, which include digital bank SoFi and Memphis-based, 157-year-old bank First Horizon, have sped up 85% of their deposit transfers so they’re done in a day, compared with the three to four days it typically takes. They have also seen a 40% reduction in instances of “NSF” or insufficient funds. An NSF is when a customer tries to move money, but by the time the transaction is about to finalize, there isn’t enough money left in the account—like a bounced check. NSFs are bad for everyone, typically triggering a $35 fee for the consumer, a $5 fee for the financial institution and, if they happen often enough, causing Nacha to cut off a bank or fintech’s access to the ACH network.
 Orum’s second product, “Momentum,” is like a control center that directs which payment rails a transaction should travel on given the financial institution’s preferences for speed and cost. When a consumer initiates a transfer, Momentum routes it intelligently, whether that’s over ACH, a credit card network, banks’ new Real Time Payments Network or even, eventually, a blockchain-based settlement system.
 Orum went live with its first customer in September 2020 and is processing tens of millions of dollars in transactions a month. Its clients pay prices ranging from $5,000 to $25,000 a month for the service.
 The New York company is still at an early stage and faces many challenges. Technical integrations with financial institutions can be complicated and must take place “over a series of sprints” with Orum’s tech team, Kirkpatrick says. Orum has a waitlist of 50 clients.
 Another challenge could arise if the U.S. government launched a central bank digital currency or “digital dollar” that dramatically sped up payments in America, reducing the need for Orum.
 Matt Harris, an Orum investor and partner at Bain Capital, says the “most aggressive” implementation of a digital dollar would be where every American has a bank account at the Federal Reserve and could move his money instantly. Thousands of financial services companies would be at risk in that scenario, Harris says. We’d no longer need banks for cash management, Visa or MasterCard for payments, Venmo for money transfers. “I don't think that's what the Fed has in mind,” Harris says. “They’ve said they are not here to destroy the banking industry.” He thinks digital dollar accounts would be limited to a few purposes, like disbursement of government funds for stimulus payments.
 Follow me on  Twitter or  LinkedIn.  Check out my  website.  Send me a secure  tip.
title: ORUM通过使用AI评估风险来加速ACH银行支付，由Bain Capital提高2100万美元的系列
title_en: Orum, which speeds up ACH bank payments by using AI to assess risk, raises $21M Series A led by Bain Capital

（路透社） - 社交媒体公司Facebook公司暂停了16,000次账户，用于在英国竞争监管后第二次在其平台上销售或购买产品和服务的假审查，这是监管机构。
 基于Facebook的Facebook还进一步更改，以检测，删除和防止有偿内容，这可能会在其平台上误导用户，包括广泛的照片共享应用程序Instagram，英国的竞争和市场管理局（CMA）在星期五表示。
  “我们与CMA广泛参与，以解决这个问题。 Facebook代表表示，我们的平台上不允许在我们的平台上允许欺诈和欺骗活动，包括提供或交易假审查。
 从2019年开始，CMA开始镇压FALSE审查当它首次提出Facebook和电子商务平台eBay Inc以检查他们的网站，并在发现误导顾客评论的日益增长的市场上的证据之后。
  Facebook也受到CMA的审查，旨在对技术公司收购GIF网站Giphy的反垄断问题。它已经迫在眉睫，世界为其数据分享做法以及假新闻和仇恨言论。
 “大流行意味着越来越多的人在线购买，以及数百万我们阅读审查，使我们在我们购物时做出明智的选择。这就是假人和误导性评论的原因如此损害，“CMA首席执行官安德烈·科斯切罗说。
 CMA在Facebook上的镇静与英国努力在监管机构内设立专门的数字市场单位，以特别关注管理数字平台。
note_en:
(Reuters) - Social media company Facebook Inc suspended 16,000 accounts for selling or buying fake reviews of products and services on its platforms, after the Britain’s competition watchdog intervened for the second time, the regulator said.
 U.S.-based Facebook also made further changes to detect, remove and prevent paid content which could mislead users on its platforms, including popular photo-sharing app Instagram, UK’s Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) said on Friday.
  “We have engaged extensively with the CMA to address this issue. Fraudulent and deceptive activity is not allowed on our platforms, including offering or trading fake reviews,” a Facebook representative said.
 The CMA began a crackdown on false reviews from 2019 when it first asked Facebook and e-commerce platform eBay Inc to check their websites after it found evidence of a growing marketplace for misleading customer reviews on the platforms.
  Facebook has also been under scrutiny by the CMA for antitrust concerns over the technology company’s acquisition of GIF website Giphy. It has been under pressure the world over for its data sharing practices as well as fake news and hate speech.
 “The pandemic has meant that more and more people are buying online, and millions of us read reviews to enable us to make informed choices when we shop around. That’s why fake and misleading reviews are so damaging,” said CMA Chief Executive Andrea Coscelli.
 CMA’s crackdown on Facebook coincides with Britain’s efforts to set up a dedicated digital markets unit within the regulatory authority to specifically look at governing digital platforms.
title: Facebook轴16,000账户在英国介入后交易假审查
title_en: Facebook axes 16,000 accounts for trading fake reviews after UK intervenes

note:
2010年，Photoshop普遍存在。无论您是编辑照片，都在编辑一张照片，还是设计网站，它发生在Photoshop中。
 今天，Adobe看起来非常强烈。由于对已经支付的大胆赌注进行了清洁的管理，他们已经有壮观的股票表现。他们到SaaS的过渡已经是无缝的，公共市场对他们来说令人享有奖励。他们在历史上看了M＆amp的最佳公司之一;他们的产品阵容是他们获得新产品系列的能力，并将其整合到它们的多产品生态系统中。也许最重要的是最不赞赏的，他们已经大大加快了其内部产品开发过程和反馈回路的节奏。与Microsoft一样，他们已成功从一家年度（或更长）发布计划运营的传统公司转移到一个真正的云公司以亚每周速度运输更新。
 尽管如此，有几个设计的部分，他们不再是市场领导者。如图，素描和帆布等公司是能够成为顶级产品的产品，尽管Adobe在所有内容设计中都有顶级产品。 Figma在2019年首次出现了Adobe的年度报告。他们在2020年重复，我并不难以努力继续进入它。
 我们该如何理解这些市场过渡以及为什么这些年轻的公司能够茁壮成长，即使是像Adobe这样的强大现任？
 这些公司从Adobe有明显的原子概念。其产品建立的原始原因与Adobe的产品阵容根本不同。这是这些不同的基本原子概念，使Adobe成为一个已建立的产品和现有UserBase的优势成为阻碍了他们对抗这些初学者的能力的弱点。这些新的原子概念来茁壮成长的机会是由新用例和在市场转型期间出土的用户类型的推动。
 了解市场转型的阶段以及驱动器的阶段是一个值得审查的普遍过程。
  对于大多数市场而言，有利于现任的优势。随着公司到达客户的偏好前沿，市场汇集了市场。这留下了很少的潜在能量，以利用新的初创公司。 
通过蛮力闯入市场并不是不可能的，但它很难。很难。大多数成功的公司，尤其是初创公司，已经找到了尾部的线程，帮助他们前进。
 改变客户需求是市场中最大的熵源。当客户需要迅速变化时，现任者的优势较少。相反，遗留公司留下了所有的开销和产品，不再是客户想要的。
 改变客户需求的原因很多。通常有不同用例的客户有新的和越来越多的段。现有产品可能为他们工作，但它们并不是理想的。他们关心的功能以及它们如何将它们与客户习惯于客户的价值截然不同。公司抵抗其产品的核心部分，因为每一个新的用例都是工作，金钱和关注的昂贵。但是每次偶尔，一旦小用例都会成长为一个足够大的东西来支持自己的公司。
 其他时候，市场转移的规模或动态足以让产品不再有效，尽管有很好的契合。公司经常被这些情况平息，因为他们已经成功完成了多年的事情突然开始动摇 - 他们并不确定为什么。 eBay是一个很好的例子。当在网上销售的物品稀缺时，他们分散的拍卖模式在新兴的互联网经济中非常好。一旦电子商务变得普遍，价格和速度变得更加重要的因素，eBay的分散模式处于劣势。亚马逊在这次流动性生态系统中建立规模经济的建设经济。
 另一个来源是客户自己改变的时候。通常，工具的功能保持不变，但用户的类型更改。这些新型客户经常有不同的东西，他们关心并导致产品需求。
 互联网驱动了全新的设计用例。 Photoshop是为编辑照片和图像而构建的。它是一个在像素级别运行的强大工具。但是，这些新的许多用途都不是图像操作。图像是一个组成部分 - 不是工作用户的本质正在努力实现。
 对于某些用户来说，这是设计数字产品。软件公司或任何与网站的公司的设计人员想要创建他们工作的网站和软件产品。这对图像操纵较少，更多关于设计这些数字产品的UI和UX。矢量比光栅图形更重要。设计这些高价值设计的复杂性和过程也变得越来越复杂。这些设计师与其他设计师和非设计师的团队合作。他们的设计是更大的产品开发过程的一部分，而且不仅仅是制作设计的重要性，而且如何改进整个过程，以更好地提高协作和切换设计。迭代地。 
设计代码中的设计和组件的复杂性也变得更加复杂。他们的工具需要对组件和变体的更高级别了解变得更加重要。设计用于了解与生成的最终产品中的HTML和CSS相同的概念和抽象级别越来越有用。
 对于某些用户来说，这是为社交平台，数字广告甚至婚礼邀请设计内容。这些通常在Photoshop中进行，但同样，像素是错误的抽象级别。图像不是唯一的组件;它们只是一个更大的设计，包括图形，文本等。同样，客户非常不同。现在，许多人在做什么，实质上，设计工作并不认为自己是设计师。他们只有一个非常具体的东西，他们想要创造，可能是可能的最少的摩擦力。
 互联网大大缩放了设计新用例的音量和类型。在许多方面，这有助于Adobe。使用Instagram等平台，编辑照片的人数已经扩大了许多数量级。在像Instagram这样的平台上编辑可能会增加显着增加，Adobe一直是互联网的巨大受益者，而云的转变 - 他们的股价是迄今为止的估约。
 [kk注意：像Instagram将编辑器中的平台捆绑在他们的社交平台上，从底部进食到Lightroom非常值得自己的讨论。也许有人会说服迈克克里格，以做出明确的作品。]
  视频中的甚至更真实。由于录制视频的能力变得普遍存在，并且视频是默认格式的平台，有更多的视频创建者作为录制视频的能力。更加醒目，许多主要的视频平台 - 就像Youtube  - 纯粹的分布集中。它们甚至没有任何编辑能力。相反，像Adobe这样的公司最终成为这种需要的大受益者。
 [kk注意：像youtube这样的平台仍然没有建立任何一个编辑器进入他们的平台，是*非常值得自己的讨论。我会说我们永远不会知道可能是什么，但后来我看着Tiktok，一切都是对世界的。]
 但是Adobe没有抓住它。在许多新的紧急用例和客户类型中，Adobe已经失去了新的创业公司。 
最好的产品地图，以如何考虑他们的工作流程。它们与客户的抽象级别相匹配：不太高，这是不可用的，但不太低，以至于很难轻易使用或以更复杂的方式扩展。
  这些是整个产品构建的核心概念。它们不仅与客户如何考虑其工作流程，而且常常为客户结晶它们应该如何结晶。伟大的原子概念是磨练的，然后在更复杂的化合物中延伸，建立在......缺乏更好的单词......化合物。
 类似的公司通常具有略微不同的原子概念，最终使它们有意义地截然不同。 Photoshop专注于像素和图像。它的重点是编辑图像和图片。其功能通过将它们转换在像素级别上来运行。
 Illustrator类似，但它在向量上运行，而不是像素。这是一个更高的级别抽象。两者都没有更好或更差，它们更适合不同的用例。 Photoshop更适合修改图像，而Illustrator是为无缝缺陷载体最佳的设计而设计的。
 素描，如插画家是基于矢量。但是专为建立数字产品而言，这意味着在项目级别运行的东西。它不是单独的设计，而是制定整个产品和用户界面 - 以及固有的重复性和一致性的需求。
 Figma建立在素描的方法上，而且还包括更重要的是，不仅仅是项目，而是整个协作过程作为相关范围。同样，它还处理更高的抽象，如插件，社区，以及更重要的概念。
 Canva类似于Photoshop和Illustrator，但其用户不是关心低级工具的设计师。相反，Canva的核心原子概念是在不同的模板和组件周围，以帮助他们轻松完成他们正在做的工作。他们正在努力的设计并不是在制作数字产品的项目水平。它们是包含图像和设计的画布。 
原子概念从根本上与公司的核心环联系起来。扩展或更改这些环路通常涉及到公司的原子概念的词汇，或者以更复杂的方式将它们添加在一起。
 紧急用例和新客户类型导致新的理想原子概念。这些新的工作流和不同客户的优先级不同于现有客户。它们如何考虑他们的问题和重量可能的解决方案是不同的，即使最终输出通常具有相似性。当然，精明的读者将在此撤销因果关系。新类型的客户是一个良好的代表，在哪里关注。但它实际上是改变的原子概念，这些概念是使初创企业对空间中的现任者的令人信服的竞争者。
 客户不关心您的技术架构或内部组织结构。当这些不再与他们想要做的工作保持一致时，那么公司的所有蔓延都变得有害，而且没有帮助。这些是核心基岩，对公司更加困难的难以改变中间飞行。使成熟公司强大的一切都建立在这个基础之上，并将反对改变它们。占据大批准及其对物理商店和延迟费用的依赖。人们经常陷入容易的叙述中，现任者在车轮上睡着了。他们太愚蠢，无法看到即将到来的威胁。这可能是真的，但这不是最常见的原因。与普遍的信念相反，磅塞的许多股票不仅看到了Netflix的威胁，而且遭到了大片的机会，以便现在举行地幔Netflix。他们甚至削弱了一个团队才能接受netflix头。但是，零售商店和延迟费用如此强大和盈利的延迟费用也是让他们如此难以取代的原因。为数字未来做准备的每一项搬家都是由产生更多收入，商店运营商在被削减的储存，而华尔街投资者对高风险企业转变为高风险企业的储存运营商。
 罕见的是可以改变其核心原子概念的公司。这就像亚马逊这样的公司是如此令人印象深刻的令人兴奋的原因。启动通过查找他们无法遵循的现任者的不对称角度而茁壮成长。什么是一个没有神圣奶牛的公司安全？
 了解公司的核心抽象级别很难从远处理解。这就是为什么寻找具有不同需求的紧急客户类型是一个有用的替代品。
  素描是公司首先了解设计数字产品的市场机会。在2010年推出，素描完全建立了设计这些产品的UI和UX。其原子概念是最适合数码产品的概念：向量和项目。这些也是难以与其预先存在的产品线竞争的原因。
 在经典的创新者的困境中，素描对抗Adobe的最佳特点是它丢弃了最不适合制作数字产品的一切。这允许它仅关注创造基于向量的数字设计的最佳体验。与Photoshop不同，它是基于矢量。与Illustrator不同，它建于较大的复杂项目，作为焦点而不是特定的孤立设计。 
回想起来，草图在半尺寸下停止。设计人员创建数字产品确实需要基于向量的设计工具。和素描还可以理解，他们正在研究更复杂的项目与一个需要更好的项目的设计，更好的项目。但这些设计师也经常与其他设计师一起工作，更重要的是，与非设计师。他们没有孤立地设计，而是作为更大过程的一部分。
 在这个领域缺少它之前的素描，就像Adobe一样。从素描的技术架构和基于桌面的产品到其定价模型和平台结构的一切都是这种合作的不良契约。这些特征的需求可以以凌乱的方式看到，公司在一起的杂乱方式以及涌现的许多产品来填充这些孔。像Zeplin，Sympli和Invision这样的公司逃离了设计人员，以便更好地与他们合作的其他设计师，PM和工程师协调。素描的插件系统，如Adobe的系统，比核心更闪烁到平台上。
 当Figma首次启动时，它更直接为Photoshop竞争对手。然而，在前两年的前两年，他们专注于在与更多潜在用户交谈的数字产品上的UI和UX的设计师。构建产品以使合作唯一的是这些设计人员的关键。这样做是非微不足道的。由于evan华莱士的技术实力和WebGL这样的新技术的特定知识，因此Figma是非常艰难的技术挑战非常艰难。建立合作以最大程度的合作使得重新思考几乎所有公司 - 导致新的定价模型，分销模式和共享形式因素。
 对于有兴趣阅读更多图形的人，我有一个可以在这里找到的先前帖子，所以避免重新恢复许多相同的观察结果。 Figma的成功来到了由更大的设计师和非设计师建造的复杂数码产品的不断增长的用例，以及寻找这种新用户偏差所需的原子概念。
 正如为什么Fogma WINS所讨论的那样，在过去的几年中，这在扩展到较大的企业客户方面最可见。大公司具有相同的（如果不更大）需要为在其ORG中为其中的协作构建的设计工具，因为它们内部的小型启动或更小的团队。但是，他们需要的一组功能和工具，这看起来与小型团队非常不同。当Figma开始时，它首先与小型团队合适，但随着整个大公司开始认真地看待它，需要了解如何考虑合作和建立一个设计工具，而不仅仅是在团队级别 - 但在规模中整个公司。
  随着像Facebook，Instagram和YouTube这样的数字平台的兴起，营销和广告越来越多地转移。在线广告与传统广告有许多差异。最值得注意的是，它的节奏更快 - 并且往往更具目标。现在，公司在同一个广告系列中做了许多小变化：测试哪种版本最佳，为不同的客户队列制作个性化版本，并将其调整到每个广告平台的不同必需的形式因素。每年拥有一些大型活动的传统过程看起来越来越古老。节奏是主要渠道是电视和印刷品的主要渠道的函数，广告系列昂贵，所以只有几个大型广告系列可以运行一年。随着频道转移，广告系列，工具和团队调整以匹配新动态。
  营销团队越来越需要在每个广告系列上工作的整个设计团队。相反，他们希望工具使他们能够轻松地以小型方式调整他们的营销设计，就像能够为他们的Instagram广告以及YouTube横幅格式化它。也需要执行此类变化的人的背景。公司在房子里携带这项工作，而不是招聘设计机构，这两者都是因为更多的工作可以由非设计者完成，因为迭代的步伐使外部机构更加慢。 
在Instagram上发布的营销人员和人们不会想到他们想要在像素方面所做的设计工作。这是错误的抽象级别。他们并不试图直接编辑照片。照片只是他们记住的具体目标的一个方面。他们就设计的美学和目的而言，他们认为 - 不仅仅是图像，还包括文本和图形等。
 Photoshop可以尽一切努力，但它太低了。 Photoshop的原子概念是图像和像素。在像素级别编辑是完美的照片和图像操作。 Canva在更高的抽象级别运行 - 其用户关心的一个。 Canva Designs的目的在于设计，无论是设计音高甲板，Instagram帖子还是婚礼邀请。 CANVA具有模板和布局，用于该特定目的，同时使用户可以轻松添加自己的创造力，无论是通过自己的照片还是使用社区所做的众多图形和组件中的任何一个。
 SMBS和团队更具必要的感觉甚至更具无法在每个项目上有完整的设计团队工作的团队。 Canva的轻量级编辑具有轻松模板和过程，可以为不同的社交平台进行格式化，使其成为这些客户的理想选择。
 这也允许Canva在这些分子水平周围扩展其平台。 Canva的分销由他们的SEO大部分驱动。不出所料，人们使用CANVA的相同用例是寻找设计工具的人们想要做和搜索Google。使用其产品和模板围绕这些用例，Canva可以轻松地公开外部，并有很多模板和实例，准备好寻求做出特定设计的潜在新用户。关于他们的用户采集和船上的一切都是由人们拥有的特定用例和Canva的原子概念构建的。它们围绕着人们拥有的功能工作流程，无论是制作推特背景照片，婚礼邀请还是主题演示。和Canva致力于尽可能简单。
   随着他们的成长，Canva通过创建模板，布局，字体等周围的市场和社区来扩展生态系统。大多数用户不想从头开始构建。使用Canva的Marketlaces，可以使用自由和付费的预制组件的整个生态系统。

note_en:
In 2010, Photoshop was ubiquitous. Whether you were editing a photo, making a poster, or designing a website, it happened in Photoshop.
 Today, Adobe looks incredibly strong. They’ve had spectacular stock performance, thanks to clear-eyed management who’ve made bold bets that have paid off. Their transition to SaaS has been seamless, for which the public markets have rewarded them handsomely. And they’re historically one of the  best companies at M&A; their product lineup is a testament to their ability to acquire new product lines and integrate them well into their multi-product ecosystem. Perhaps most importantly and least appreciated, they have dramatically sped up the cadence of their internal product development process and feedback loop. Like Microsoft, they have successfully shifted from a legacy company operating on an annual (or longer) release schedule to a truly cloud company shipping updates at a sub-weekly pace.
 Nevertheless, there are a few segments of design where they’re no longer the market leader. Companies like Figma, Sketch, and Canva are examples of products that have been able to become top products despite Adobe’s ubiquity in all things design. Figma showed up in Adobe’s annual report for the first time in  2019. They reprised in  2020, and I’m not uncertain they will continue to be in it going forward.
 How should we understand these market transitions and why these young companies are able to thrive, even against a strong incumbent like Adobe?
 These companies have distinct atomic concepts from Adobe. The primitives that their products are built around are fundamentally different from those of Adobe’s product lineup. It’s these different fundamental atomic concepts that turn Adobe’s advantage of an established product and existing userbase into a weakness that hinders their ability to counter these upstarts. The opportunity for these new atomic concepts to thrive is driven by the new use cases and types of users unearthed during market transitions.
 Understanding the phases of market transition and what drives them is a universal process worth examining.
  For most markets, there are advantages to being an incumbent. Markets converge as companies arrive at the preference frontier of customers. This leaves little potential energy for new startups to take advantage of.
  It’s not impossible to break into a market by brute force, but it’s hard. Very hard. Most successful companies, especially startups, have found tailwinds to harness that help pull them forward.
 Changing customer needs are the largest source of entropy in markets. When customer needs rapidly change, there is less advantage in being an incumbent. Instead, legacy companies are left with all the overhead and a product that no longer is what customers want.
 There are many causes of changing customer needs. Often there are new and growing segments of customers with different use cases. Existing products may work for them, but they aren’t ideal. The features they care about and how they value them are very different from the customers the legacy company is used to. Companies resist changing core parts of their product for every new use case since it’s costly in work, money, and attention. But every once in a while, what was once a small use case grows into one large enough to support its own company.
 Other times the scale or dynamics of a market shift enough to make a product no longer work despite having been a great fit. Companies are often caught flat-footed by these situations because what they have done successfully for years suddenly starts to falter—and they aren’t sure why. Ebay is a good example of this. Their decentralized auction model was very good in a nascent internet economy when there was a scarcity of items being sold online. Once ecommerce became commonplace, price and speed became much more important factors and Ebay’s decentralized model was at a disadvantage. Amazon was much better at building economies of scale in this post-liquidity ecosystem.
 Another source is when the customers themselves change. Often the function of a tool remains the same, but the type of user changes. These new types of customers often have different things they care about and resulting product needs.
 The internet drove entirely new design use cases. Photoshop was built for editing photos and images. It’s a powerful tool that operates at the pixel level. However, many of these new uses weren’t about image manipulation. Images were a component—not the essence—of the job users were trying to accomplish.
 For some users, this was designing digital products. Designers at software companies or any company with a website wanted to create the websites and software products they worked on. This is less about image manipulation and more about designing the UI and UX of these digital products. Vectors are more important than raster graphics. The complexity and process of designing these high-value designs also got increasingly more sophisticated. These designers worked with teams of other designers and non-designers. Their designs are part of a larger product development process and what mattered wasn’t just making a design, but how that the entire process could be improved to make collaboration easier and handoff of designs better. Iteratively.
 The complexity of the designs and the components in the resulting code became more complex, too. The need for their tools to have a higher-level understanding of the components and variants became more important. It’s increasingly useful for designs to understand the same concepts and abstraction levels as the HTML and CSS in the resulting end product.
 For some users, this was designing content for social platforms, digital ads, or even wedding invitations. These were often made in Photoshop, but again, pixels are the wrong abstraction level. Images are not the sole component; they are just past of a larger design that includes graphics, text, and more. Similarly, the customers are very different. Many of the people now doing what is, in essence, design work don’t think of themselves as designers. They just have a very specific thing they want to create, with the least friction possible.
 The internet dramatically scales up the volume and type of new use cases for design. In many ways, this helps Adobe. With platforms like Instagram, the number of people editing photos has expanded by many orders of magnitude. While editing on platforms like Instagram may have increased significantly, Adobe has been a huge beneficiary of the internet and the shift to cloud—and their  stock price is a testament to this.
 [KK Note: Platforms like Instagram strapping editors onto their social platforms and eating into Lightroom from the bottom up is well worth its own discussion. And perhaps someone will convince Mike Krieger to do the definitive piece on that.]
  This is even more true in video. There are orders of magnitude more video creators as the ability to record video has become ubiquitous and the platforms where video is the default format have grown. Even more striking, many of the dominant video platforms—like Youtube—are purely distribution focused. They don’t even have any editing capabilities. Instead, companies like Adobe end up being large beneficiaries of this need.
 [KK Note: Platforms like Youtube still having not built any semblance of an editor into their platform is *also* well worth its own discussion. I’d say we’ll never know what could be, but then I look at TikTok and all is right with the world.]
 But Adobe hasn’t captured it all. And in many of these new emergent use cases and customer types, Adobe has lost the lead to new startups.
  The best products map to how customers think about their workflow. They match the abstraction level of their customers: not too high that it’s unusable, but not too low that it’s hard to use easily or extend in more complex ways.
  These are the core concepts around which the entire product is built. They not only align with how customers think of their workflow, but often crystallizes for customers how they ought to. Great atomic concepts are honed and then extended and built upon in more complex compounds that…well for lack of a better word… compound.
 Similar companies often have slightly different atomic concepts that end up making them meaningfully distinct. Photoshop is focused on pixels and images. Its focus is on editing images and pictures. And its functions operate by transforming them on a pixel level.
 Illustrator is similar, but it operates on vectors, not pixels. This is a higher level abstraction. Neither is better or worse, they are just more suited to different use cases. Photoshop is better for modifying images, while illustrator is built for designs where scale-free vectors are best.
 Sketch, like Illustrator, is vector based. But is designed for building digital products which means things like operating at a project level. It is not individual designs, but crafting entire products and user interfaces—and the needs for repeatability and consistency inherent to that.
 Figma builds on Sketch’s approach, but also includes a greater focus on not just projects but the entire collaborative process as the relevant scope. Similarly, it also treats higher level abstractions like plugins, community, and more as equally important concepts.
 Canva is similar to Photoshop and Illustrator, but its users aren’t designers who care about low level tools. Instead Canva’s core atomic concepts are around the different templates and components to help them easily accomplish the job they are doing. And the designs they are working on are not quite at the project level of making a digital product. They are canvases that include images and design.
  Atomic concepts are fundamentally linked to the core loops of a company. Expanding or changing these loops often involves adding to a company’s vocabulary of atomic concepts or adding them together in more complex ways.
 Emergent use cases and new customer types lead to new ideal atomic concepts. These new workflows and different customers have different priorities than existing customers. How they think about their problems and weight possible solutions is different, even if often the end output has similarities. Of course, astute readers will pick up that causality is reversed here. New types of customers are a good proxy for where to pay attention. But it is  actually the changed atomic concepts that are what make startups a compelling contender against incumbents in the space.
 Customers don’t care about your technical architecture or internal org structure. When these no longer align with the job they are trying to do, then all the sprawl of the company becomes harmful, not helpful. These are the core bedrock that are much more difficult for a company to change mid-flight. Everything that makes an established company strong is built on top of this foundation and will fight back against changing them. Take Blockbuster and its reliance on physical stores and late fees. People often fall into the easy narrative that incumbents are asleep at the wheel. That they are too stupid to see the coming threat. This can be true but it isn’t the most common reason. Contrary to popular belief, many execs at Blockbuster not only saw the threat Netflix posed, but also the opportunity for Blockbuster to have claimed the mantle Netflix now holds. They even spun up a team to take Netflix head on. But what made retail stores and late fees so powerful and profitable for Blockbuster is also what made them so hard to displace. Every move to prepare Blockbuster’s core for a digital future was resisted by execs who generated more revenue, store operators who were livid at being cut out, and Wall Street investors uncomfortable with turning a consistent business into a high risk venture.
 Rare is the company that can change its core atomic concepts. It’s why companies like Amazon are so impressive and so daunting. Startups thrive by finding asymmetric angles on incumbents that they are unable to follow. What is safe from a company with no sacred cows?
 Understanding the core abstraction levels of a company is hard to understand from a distance. Which is why looking for emergent customer types with different needs is a useful substitute.
  Sketch was the company to first understand the market opportunity in designing digital products. Launched in 2010, Sketch was built entirely for designing the UI and UX of these products. Its atomic concepts were those best for digital products: vectors and projects. These were also what made it hard for Adobe to compete with their pre-existing product line.
 In a classic innovator’s dilemma, Sketch’s best feature against Adobe was that it dropped everything that wasn’t best for making digital products. This allowed it to focus only on creating the best experience for vector-based digital design. Unlike Photoshop, it was vector based. And unlike Illustrator it was built with larger complex projects as the focus rather than specific isolated designs.
 In retrospect, Sketch stopped at a half measure. Designers creating digital products did need vector-based design tools. And Sketch also understood that they were working on more complex projects vs one off designs that needed better project-first features. But these designers were  also often working on teams—both with other designers and, more importantly, with non-designers. They weren’t designing in isolation, but as part of a larger process.
 Sketch, like Adobe before it, lacked in this area. Everything from Sketch’s technical architecture and desktop based product to its pricing model and platform structure were a poor fit for this collaboration. The demand for these features could be seen in the messy ways that companies hacked together solutions to this and the many products that sprung up to fill these holes. Companies like Zeplin, Sympli, and Invision grew out of designers’ needs for better ways to coordinate with the other designers, PMs, and engineers they worked with. Sketch’s plugin system, like Adobe’s, felt more bolted on than core to the platform.
 When Figma first started, it was more directly a Photoshop competitor. Over its first two years, though, they shifted their focus specifically to designers working on the UI and UX of digital products as they talked to more potential users. Building out the product to enable collaboration uniquely was key to these designers. Doing this was non-trivial. The technical challenges to do so were very hard, though Figma was well set up due to Evan Wallace’s technical prowess and specific knowledge in new technologies like WebGL. Building for collaboration to its fullest extent has led Figma to rethink almost all of the company—leading to new pricing models, distribution models, and sharing form factors.
 For those interested in reading more on Figma, I have a prior post that can be found  here so will avoid rehashing many of the same observations. Figma’s success came as it honed in on this growing use case of complex digital products built by larger teams of designers and non-designers—and in finding the atomic concepts that were uniquely needed for this new skew of users.
 As discussed in  Why Figma Wins, over the last few years this is most visible in their expansion into larger enterprise customers. Large companies have the same (if not greater) need for design tools that are built for the collaboration in their org as small startups or smaller teams within them. However, the set of features and tools they need around this look very different from a small team. When Figma started, it found its fit first with small teams, but as entire large companies started to look at it seriously it needed to understand how to think about collaboration and building a design tool not just at a team level—but at the scale of an entire company.
  With the rise of digital platforms like Facebook, Instagram, and Youtube, marketing and advertising have increasingly shifted online. Online advertising has many differences from traditional advertising. Most notably, it is much faster paced—and often more targeted. Companies now do many small variations on the same campaign: testing which versions do best, making personalized versions for different customer cohorts, and adjusting them to the different required form factors of each ad platform. The traditional process of having a few large campaigns each year looks increasingly archaic. The cadence was a function of the primary channels being areas like TV and print, where campaigns are costly so only a few large campaigns can be run a year. As the channels shift, the campaigns, tools, and teams adjust to match the new dynamics.
  Increasingly, marketing teams don’t need whole design teams working on each campaign. Rather, they want tools that made it easy for them to adjust their marketing designs in small ways—like being able to format it for both their instagram ad as well as their Youtube banner. The background of the person needed to do this changes, too. Instead of hiring design agencies, companies bring this work in house, both because more of the work can be done by non-designers and because the pace of iterations makes working with an external agency too slow.
  Marketers and people posting on Instagram don’t think of the design work they want to do in terms of pixels. It’s the wrong abstraction level. They aren’t trying to directly edit the photos themselves. The photos are just an aspect of the specific goal they have in mind. They think of it in terms of the aesthetics and purpose of the design—not just the images but also the text and graphics and more.
 Photoshop can do everything they want, but it is too low level. Photoshop’s atomic concepts are images and pixels. Editing at the pixel level is perfect for photos and image manipulation. Canva operates at a higher abstraction level—the one its users care about. Canva designs start with their purpose in mind, whether that’s designing a pitch deck, an Instagram post, or a wedding invitation. Canva has templates and layouts built for that specific purpose, while making it easy for users to add their own creativity, whether by putting in their own photos or using any of the many graphics and components made by the community.
 This need is even more felt by SMBs and teams who can’t have a full design team work on every project. Canva’s lightweight editing with easy templates and process for making many small changes like formatting for different social platforms made it ideal for these customers.
 This also allows Canva to extend its platform around these molecular levels. Canva’s distribution is driven in large part by their SEO. Unsurprisingly, the very same use cases people use Canva for are what people looking for design tools want to do and search Google for. With their product and templates built around these use cases, it’s easy for Canva to expose that externally and have lots of templates and examples ready to go for potential new users looking to do a specific design. Everything about their user acquisition and onboarding is built around the specific use cases people have and Canva’s atomic concepts. They are built around the functional workflows people have, whether that’s making a Twitter background photo, a wedding invite, or a keynote presentation. And Canva is committed to making that as easy as possible.
   As they’ve grown, Canva has expanded their ecosystem by creating marketplaces and communities around templates, layouts, fonts, and more. Most users don’t want to build from scratch. With Canva’s marketplaces there is an entire ecosystem of pre-built components they can use, both free and paid.
 Canva having this strong ecosystem of add-ons is very powerful. Add-ons allow Canva to address the huge scale and varied needs of all its customers, far more than one company could ever do on its own. T
